subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Logical progression from introduction through methodology, analysis, and evaluation
Clear structure supported by headings, table of contents, numbered equations, and labeled figures
Transitional sentences effectively link sections, maintaining narrative coherence
weaknesses: Duplicate section headings (âDeriving the Heat Equationâ) disrupt flow
Figures lack complete axis labels and units, hindering precise interpretation
Unnecessary duplication of derivations and overly detailed tables in main text
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed with a well-organized structure (band 3). While transitional elements and sign-posting are strong, minor lapsesâincluding duplicated headings, missing labels on figures, and occasional digressionsâprevent it from reaching the âexceptionally well organizedâ level. A score of 3 appropriately reflects this performance.

strengths: Mostly consistent use of appropriate mathematical notation (variables, subscripts, integrals)
Effective employment of diagrams and regression plots that support the discussion
Clear, step-by-step layout of derivations with linking sentences
weaknesses: Inconsistent use of Î´ versus â in Fourierâs law notation
Missing axis labels and units in several plots impede full clarity
Occasional mis-typed derivative subscripts (e.g., âÂ²T/âtÂ² instead of âÂ²T/ârÂ²)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Mathematical language, notation, and representations are generally effective and logically structured (band 3). Minor notation slips and incomplete figure labeling limit the work from reaching full accuracy and clarity throughout, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: Engaging personal anecdote (dosa cooking) demonstrates genuine interest
Original construction of experimental apparatus shows initiative and personal engagement
weaknesses: Approach largely follows standard separation-of-variables procedures without deeper innovation
Mathematical modeling choices are competent but not highly inventive
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows significant independent thinking and a clear personal approach, particularly in designing the experiment (band 2). However, the work does not reach outstanding levels of originality or creativity, so a score of 2 accurately reflects the level of personal engagement.

strengths: Meaningful reflection on experimental errors and mathematical limitations throughout
Thoughtful analysis of discrepancies between theoretical and experimental diffusivity
Clear evaluation of outcomes with suggestions for future improvements
weaknesses: Reflection remains more descriptive than deeply critical in places
Justification for model choices (e.g., sine regression) lacks depth
Limited discussion of uncertainty margins and impact prioritization
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is present at key stages with thoughtful analysis and evaluation (band 2). While the student identifies limitations and proposes improvements, the critical depth and forward-looking implications do not yet reach the highest tier, supporting a score of 2.

strengths: Relevant application of PDEs, separation of variables, Fourier series, and regression analysis
Mathematics employed is commensurate with SL course level and well integrated into the investigation
Demonstrates a good understanding of theory and its link to experimental data
weaknesses: Frequent algebraic/sign errors (incorrect cancellations, sign conventions) that propagate through derivations
Numerical inconsistencies (mismatched coefficients, negative thermal diffusivity) indicate lapses in accuracy
Key assumptions (separability, boundary conditions) are insufficiently justified
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the mathematics is relevant, appropriately leveled, and generally well integrated, pervasive minor errors and inconsistencies reduce the overall accuracy and depth of understanding (band 3). Consequently, a score of 3 reflects the solid but imperfect use of mathematics in this SL exploration.

strandSelections.reasoning: Derivations are laid out step-by-step and numbered, with brief linking sentences explaining each step.  Although a few jumps in logic occur (e.g. incorrect cancellation of terms when limits applied), the bulk of the argument is understandable and logically sequenced, meeting the âgood logical structure and clear communicationâ description.

Derivations exhibit generally sound understanding, yet there are notable slips: cancelling terms incorrectly when forming limits, sign errors in b, and a mismatch between derived and experimental temperature at (2, 5).  These indicate minor but non-trivial errors, so the work evidences âgood understanding with minor errorsâ rather than total correctness.

The student employs tables of raw data, two scatter plots with regression curves, a schematic of the plate and concentric-circle diagram.  These representations are appropriate and clearly linked to the discussion, though axis labels and scales are occasionally omitted.  The use is therefore âeffectiveâ rather than âhighly effectiveâ.

The exploration moves from introduction, background theory, methodology, data collection, analysis and finally evaluation in a sequence that is easy to follow.  Transitional sentences generally link sections and the mathematical narrative builds logically from Fourierâs law to the studentâs model.  Although a few derivations are revisited later and there is some duplication, the overall flow remains coherent and clearly purposeful, justifying a band in the âcoherent and logically developedâ level rather than only âsome coherenceâ.

Initiative is shown in constructing the physical set-up, selecting regression models and attempting a Fourier-series solution, yet these choices are standard techniques for the problem.  Creativity is therefore good but not âexceptionalâ across the whole work.

Differences between theoretical and experimental diffusivity are quantified (13.1 %) and possible causes proposed.  While this shows thoughtful analysis, the discussion stops short of probing the validity of regression choices or boundary assumptions in depth, aligning with the âthoughtful analysisâ band.

An evaluation section lists practical and mathematical limitations and briefly suggests improvements (digital thermometers, non-homogeneous treatment).  The student does consider implications but future steps are outline-level rather than comprehensive, fitting the middle band.

The student reflects on experimental errors (heat loss, thermometer range, alloy composition) and mathematical limitations (homogeneous vs non-homogeneous boundaries).  Reflection appears at key points but is descriptive rather than deeply critical, matching âmeaningful reflection throughoutâ.

Variables, partial-derivative symbols, subscripts and integral notation are generally appropriate and readable.  Isolated slips (Î´ used in place of â and inconsistent use of capital T/u) appear but do not impede understanding.  Hence usage is âmostly consistentâ rather than wholly âaccurate throughoutâ.

The personal narrative connecting dosa cooking to heat equations and the custom-built cardboard insulation indicate a clear personal approach.  However, the remainder of the report adopts conventional derivations and software, so the approach, while evident, is not highly innovative.

The narrative sometimes digresses (extended anecdote about cooking, repeated algebra already shown, long data tables embedded in the main text).  Several derivations are reproduced at length when a concise reference would suffice.  Nevertheless, most material is relevant to modelling heat flow, so the work fits âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elementsâ.

The work draws on partial derivatives, separation of variables, Fourier series, regression analysis and experimental statistics â all clearly relevant and integrated into addressing the research question.  These tools are used in a purposeful manner that demonstrates effectiveness, though not flawless mastery, hence a band 4 assignment.

Designing and executing a primary experiment to collect temperature data, then attempting to derive thermal diffusivity from an original model, demonstrates notable independent thought.  Nevertheless, the mathematics closely follows textbook separation-of-variables procedures and does not break new conceptual ground, so the evidence is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

Headings, a table of contents, numbered equations and labelled figures/tables provide a clear skeleton.  Each major part is separated and sign-posted, enabling the reader to locate information quickly.  Minor lapses (e.g. two sections both called âDeriving the Heat Equationâ) prevent the piece from reaching the âexceptionally well organisedâ descriptor but the document is definitely âwell organised with clear structureâ.

PDEs and Fourier methods lie at the upper end of HL expectations but still within syllabus extension material; their application to an original data set is appropriate and sustained.  Some algebraic inaccuracies prevent the jump to âexpertly appliedâ, so the strand fits âfully commensurate with course levelâ.

annotations.comment: The personal anecdote linking dosa cooking to heat transfer is engaging and clearly demonstrates genuine interest and initiative in the topic.
In the assumptions list, item 2 describes a rod rather than a circular plate, which contradicts earlier context. Revise the text to maintain consistency with the 2-D plate model.
Fourierâs law equation uses the symbol Î´ instead of the partial derivative â, which deviates from standard notation. Consistent use of â would improve mathematical communication.

The table of contents includes two entries titled âDeriving the Heat Equation,â which may confuse the reader and disrupt the documentâs logical flow. Consider renaming or consolidating identical headings.
Figure 1âs schematic lacks axis labels or dimensions, making it hard to interpret the geometry quantitatively. Adding labels to the diagram would aid clarity.
The net heat flow expression substitutes Qâ and Qâ incorrectly in the final subtraction. Revisit the algebraic sign convention when summing and subtracting the heat fluxes.
The methodology demonstrates notable independent initiative by constructing a concentricâcircle measurement apparatus and planning a primary experiment.
The points-marking figure effectively shows experimental setup; it complements the text well by clarifying the data-collection procedure.
The choice of a sine regression for temperature vs. radius yields RÂ²=0.7948, but the justification for using a sin function over simpler forms is missing. Reflect on the model selection.
The regression plot on page 9 is missing axis labels and units, which impedes interpretation of results. Include complete axes in future figures.
The exponential regression of time vs. temperature shows an excellent fit (RÂ²=0.9968), effectively supporting the separation assumption T(r,t)=f(r)g(t).
Assuming separability T(r,t)=f(r)g(t) requires discussion of its mathematical validity. Provide conditions under which separation holds for this PDE.
When multiplying by 1/k in the separation step, the factor k remains on the wrong side in subsequent equations. Check the algebra when isolating variables.
Using the single data point g(6)=40.254 to find k may amplify measurement errors. Consider fitting k across multiple time points for better accuracy.
The general solution section repeatedly re-derives earlier results, causing unnecessary duplication. Streamline the narrative by referencing prior derivations.
The introduction of the separation constant âÎ± lacks justification of its sign convention. Explain why it is taken negative and its physical meaning.
In the integration for f(r), the term 22.0764/0.2936Â² appears; verify Signs and coefficients carefully, as a sign error here propagates through the Fourier coefficients.
The solved value Î±=â0.0098 is negative, contradicting the physical requirement of positive thermal diffusivity. Re-examine the derivation of Î± and constraint imposition.
The numerical value of bâ appears as 71.38 in the T(2,5) computation but was previously 17.38. Reconcile this discrepancy to avoid propagating errors.
When comparing predicted and experimental T(2,5), include uncertainty margins to contextualize the 15.82Â°C discrepancy and discuss its significance.
The reflection on transforming non-homogeneous boundaries into homogeneous ones shows substantial critical insight and indicates strong understanding of future improvements.
The second derivative term is mis-typed as âÂ²T/âtÂ² in one place; ensure correct variable subscripts (r not t) for the spatial derivative.
Your reflection on the Î±=0 case correctly notes the mismatch with experimental behavior. This demonstrates thoughtful critical reflection on model validity.
The boundary condition T(0,t)=0 is applied but the trivial solution is not excluded rigorously. Discuss why non-zero amplitude solutions exist.
Figure 5âs regression of initial condition data (RÂ²=0.9097) is effective, showing a clear decay trend appropriate for the boundary function.
The Fourier coefficient formula is stated correctly, but the subsequent substitution omits the factor 2/l in one step. Double-check the normalization factor.
The evaluation lists key experimental and mathematical limitations but could be deepened by prioritizing which issues had the greatest impact on diffusivity.
The appendices contain extensive raw data tables that overwhelm the reader. Summarize key trends in the main text and move detailed tables entirely to the back.
